title: Blueprint
text:
Single overlapping ball pair (Lemma B.2)

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Lemma B.2, p. 31).
Consider two open balls \(\mathcal B_0,\mathcal B_1\subset\mathbb S^{d-1}\) such that \(\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1\ne\varnothing\). For any \(\varepsilon>0\) and \(T>0\), there exist \(\mathbf W,\mathbf U\in\mathcal M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R)\) and \(b\in\mathbb R^d\) such that for any \(\mu_0\in\mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\), the unique solution \(\mu\) to (B.1) satisfies \[\mu(T,\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1)\ge(1-\varepsilon)\,\mu_0(\mathcal B_0).\tag{B.3}\] Moreover \(\mu(T)=\phi^T_{\#}\mu_0\) where the Lipschitz-continuous and invertible flow map \(\Phi^t:\mathbb S^{d-1}\to\mathbb S^{d-1}\) of (4.1) satisfies, for all \(t\in[0,T]\), \[(\phi^t)_{|\mathbb S^{d-1}\setminus\mathcal B_0}\equiv\operatorname{Id}.\] Furthermore, for any fixed \(\omega\in\operatorname{int}\mathcal B_0\) we can choose \(\mathbf W,\mathbf U\) and \(b\) so that the solution to (B.1) satisfies \(W_2(\mu(T),\alpha)\le\varepsilon\) where \(\alpha(A)=\mu_0(\mathcal B_0)\,\delta_\omega(A)+\mu_0(A\setminus\mathcal B_0)\), for any Borel \(A\subset\mathbb S^{d-1}\).
Proof sketch (full proof p. 32). Let \(z\) and \(R\) be the center and radius of \(\mathcal B_0\) and fix \(\omega\in\operatorname{int}(\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1)\). The authors choose \(\mathbf U,b\) and a \(\mathbf W\) with \(\mathbf W\mathbf 1=\omega\) so that the gated drift is \((-\cos d_g(z,x)+\cos R)_+\,\omega=(\cos R-\langle z,x\rangle)_+\,\omega\), and record (B.4) \((\cos R-\langle z,x\rangle)_+>0\iff x\in\mathcal B_0\) (see the strengthening below). Then (B.5) \[\tfrac{d}{dt}\langle x(t),\omega\rangle=(\cos R-\langle z,x(t)\rangle)_+\bigl(1-\langle x(t),\omega\rangle^2\bigr),\] which is positive whenever \(x(t)\in\mathcal B_0\setminus\{\omega\}\). Hence for small \(\delta>0\) there is a time \(T_\delta\) with \(\phi^{T_\delta}(x)\in\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1\) for \(x\in B(z,R-\delta)\) (B.7); pushing forward, \(\mu(T_\delta,\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1)=\mu_0\!\bigl((\phi^{T_\delta})^{-1}(\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1)\bigr)\ge\mu_0(B(z,R-\delta))\) (B.8), and taking \(\delta\) small enough that \(\mu_0(B(z,R-\delta))\ge(1-\varepsilon)\mu_0(\mathcal B_0)\) gives (B.3) after rescaling time. The gate vanishes off \(\mathcal B_0\), so \(\phi^t\equiv\operatorname{Id}\) there. The bound to \(\alpha\) follows by taking \(\mathcal B_1=B(\omega,\eta)\subset\mathcal B_0\) and a \(W_1\) Kantorovich-Rubinstein estimate, sending \(\eta\) and the slack to zero.


What we strengthen. As printed the proof takes \(\mathbf U=-\mathbf 1 z^\top\), \(b=\cos(R)\mathbf 1\) (with \(\mathbf W\mathbf 1=\omega\)), so \(\mathbf U x=-\langle z,x\rangle\mathbf 1\), giving the gate coefficient \((\cos R-\langle z,x\rangle)_+\), and asserts in (B.4) that this is positive iff \(x\in\mathcal B_0=B(z,R)\). But \[\cos R-\langle z,x\rangle>0\iff\langle z,x\rangle<\cos R\iff d_g(z,x)>R\iff x\notin\mathcal B_0,\] so as written the gate is active on the complement of \(\mathcal B_0\): the interior mass it is meant to move sees a zero gate, and the lemma transports nothing. The fix is a one-sign typo — take \(\mathbf U=+\mathbf 1 z^\top\), \(b=-\cos(R)\mathbf 1\), giving the gate \((\langle z,x\rangle-\cos R)_+\), positive exactly on \(\mathcal B_0\) — after which (B.3)-(B.5) and the rest of the argument go through and Lemma B.2 holds as stated. The corrected equivalence \(g(x)>0\iff d_g(z,x)>R\) is the kernel-checked leaf L2 (gate_pos_iff_dist, machine-checked); the same inversion is corroborated independently by Proposition 4.2, Step 3 (which states the identical construction with the correct activation side) and by experiment E1. This is finding F1; see ERRATA.md.


Our formalization. axiomatised
The authors’ strategy is the gated-drift construction above: a single ReLU gate, active on \(\mathcal B_0\) (after the F1 sign correction), pushes the interior mass of \(\mathcal B_0\) along \(+\omega\) into \(\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1\) while leaving everything off \(\mathcal B_0\) fixed, so a \((1-\varepsilon)\) fraction is retained in the overlap. We state it as a labeled axiom over the flow layer (measureFlow, switches), and — a soundness correction — over genuine geodesic balls \(\mathcal B_i=B(z_i,R_i)\) rather than arbitrary sets: the gated drift funnels a cap toward its overlap with another cap, and the retention claim is simply false for arbitrary \(B_0,B_1\) (finding F1’s companion fix). It also now carries the switches θ ≤ 1 budget the chain lemma needs.
While the axiom itself is not yet discharged (that rests on the continuity-equation flow existence Mathlib lacks), its entire dependency tree is now built and machine-checked — the ball signature is exactly what the eventual discharge will prove:

gatedBlock machine-checked — the B.5 field \(\chi(\lVert x\rVert)(\langle z,x\rangle-\cos R)_+\,\mathbf P_x^\perp\omega\) as a genuine well-posed Block: globally Lipschitz (the quadratic projector tamed by a cutoff via a bespoke vanishing-gluing lemma), bounded, and radially tangent so its flow preserves the sphere;
hasDerivAt_inner_gatedFlow machine-checked — along that flow the coordinate \(u(t)=\langle\phi^t x,\omega\rangle\) obeys the logistic gate ODE \(u'=g\,(1-u^2)\) (B.5);
logistic_flow_reach machine-checked — the finite-time reaching estimate (B.7), via a log-odds substitution whose derivative along the flow is exactly \(2g\);
exists_closed_sublevel_mass_ge machine-checked — the shrunken-cap mass bound (B.6);
inner_mem_Ioo_of_ne machine-checked — strict Cauchy-Schwarz keeping \(u\in(-1,1)\).

What remains open is the final assembly: the reaching argument on the sphere plus the B.8 measure-preimage step \(\mu(T,\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1)=\mu\bigl(\phi^{-1}(\mathcal B_0\cap\mathcal B_1)\bigr)\ge\mu(B(z_0,R_0-\delta))\).
/-- **Lemma B.2** (single ball pair). Mass in the geodesic ball `B(z₀,R₀)` is pushed into
`B(z₀,R₀) ∩ B(z₁,R₁)`, retaining `(1-ε)`, with one switch. AXIOM (`math.axiomatised`); the
discharge machinery (gatedBlock, logistic_flow_reach, …) is machine-checked. (Finding F1: the
gate sign is `U = +z 𝟙ᵀ, b = -cos(R) 𝟙`; see `ERRATA.md`.) -/
axiom lemma_B_2 (μ : Measure (Eucl d)) (T ε : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T) (hε : 0 < ε)
    (z₀ z₁ : Eucl d) (hz₀ : z₀ ∈ sphere d) (hz₁ : z₁ ∈ sphere d) (R₀ R₁ : ℝ)
    (hcap : (geodesicBall z₀ R₀ ∩ geodesicBall z₁ R₁).Nonempty) :
    ∃ θ : Params d, switches θ ≤ 1 ∧
      (1 - ENNReal.ofReal ε) * μ (geodesicBall z₀ R₀) ≤
        (measureFlow θ T μ) (geodesicBall z₀ R₀ ∩ geodesicBall z₁ R₁)

title: Blueprint
text:
Chain of overlapping balls (Lemma B.1)

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Lemma B.1, p. 31).
Consider \(K+1\) open balls \(\mathcal B_K,\dots,\mathcal B_1,\mathcal B_0\subset\mathbb S^{d-1}\) satisfying \[\mathcal B_k\cap\mathcal B_{k-1}\ne\varnothing\quad\text{for }k\in[\![1,K]\!],\qquad
\mathcal B_k\cap\mathcal B_{k'}=\varnothing\quad\text{if }|k-k'|\ge2.\] Then for any \(T>0\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist \((\mathbf W,\mathbf U,b):[0,T]\to\mathcal M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R)^2\times\mathbb R^d\), piecewise constant having at most \(K\) switches, such that for any \(\mu_0\in\mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\), the corresponding unique solution \(\mu\) to \[\begin{cases}\partial_t\mu(t)+\operatorname{div}\bigl(\mathbf P_x^\perp\,\mathbf W(t)(\mathbf U(t)x+b(t))_+\,\mu(t)\bigr)=0 & \text{on }[0,T]\times\mathbb S^{d-1},\\[2pt]\mu(0)=\mu_0 & \text{on }\mathbb S^{d-1},\end{cases}\tag{B.1}\] satisfies \[\mu(T,\mathcal B_K)\ge(1-\varepsilon)^K\,\mu_0\Bigl(\bigcup_k\mathcal B_k\Bigr).\] Moreover, \(\mu(T)=\phi^T_{\#}\mu_0\) for a Lipschitz-continuous, invertible map \(\phi^t:\mathbb S^{d-1}\to\mathbb S^{d-1}\) which satisfies, for all \(t\in[0,T]\), \[\phi^t(x)=x\quad\text{for }x\notin\bigcup_k\mathcal B_k.\tag{B.2}\]
Proof sketch (full proof p. 33). Backward induction on the chain. Write \([0,T)=\bigcup_{k\in[\![1,K]\!]}[t_{k-1},t_k)\) with \(t_k=kT/K\) and apply Lemma B.2 to the adjacent pair \((\mathcal B_{k-1},\mathcal B_k)\) on each subinterval. Then \[\mu(T,\mathcal B_K)=\mu(T,\mathcal B_K\setminus\mathcal B_{K-1})+\mu(T,\mathcal B_K\cap\mathcal B_{K-1})\ge\mu(t_{K-1},\mathcal B_K\setminus\mathcal B_{K-1})+(1-\varepsilon)\,\mu(t_{K-1},\mathcal B_{K-1}),\] the last step by Lemma B.2. The non-adjacency disjointness \(\mathcal B_k\cap\mathcal B_{k'}=\varnothing\) for \(|k-k'|\ge2\) keeps the steps from interfering, and iterating gives \[\mu(T,\mathcal B_K)\ge(1-\varepsilon)^K\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^K\mu_0(\mathcal B_k\setminus\mathcal B_{k-1})+\mu_0(\mathcal B_0)\Bigr)=(1-\varepsilon)^K\,\mu_0\Bigl(\bigcup_{k\in[\![0,K]\!]}\mathcal B_k\Bigr).\]


Our formalization. machine-checked
The authors’ argument is a backward induction that applies the single-pair lemma (Lemma B.2) once per adjacent overlap, each step retaining a factor \((1-\varepsilon)\). We prove this in Lean by induction on the chain length \(K\): the base case is the identity schedule (idParams, zero switches); each step composes one lemma_B_2 transport via comp, carrying the previous mass forward with measureFlow_comp, passing from \(\mathcal B_k\cap\mathcal B_{k+1}\) to \(\mathcal B_{k+1}\) by measure_mono, and adding the switch budget with switches_comp. So the retention bound is machine-checked given the single-ball transport fact; #print axioms lemma_B_1 lists exactly lemma_B_2 and the structural flow algebra.
Two corrections over the original type-correct stub. The retained fraction multiplies \(\mu(\mathcal B_0)\) — the mass that starts in the first ball and is funneled along the chain — not \(\mu(\bigcup_k\mathcal B_k)\) (which would make the \(K=0\) base case false, since \(\mathcal B_0\subseteq\bigcup_k\mathcal B_k\)). And the chain is a sequence of genuine geodesic balls \(B(z_k,R_k)\), matching the faithful lemma_B_2 signature. The chain-overlap hypothesis hchain and the per-step switches ≤ 1 budget are what make the bound hold.
/-- **Lemma B.1** (ball-chain retention). For a chain of `K+1` overlapping geodesic balls, `K`
switches retain `(1-ε)^K` of the mass initially in `B(z₀,R₀)` into the last ball. PROVED
(`math.axiomatised`: rests only on `lemma_B_2` + the flow algebra) by induction on `K`. -/
theorem lemma_B_1 (μ : Measure (Eucl d)) (T ε : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T) (hε : 0 < ε)
    (K : ℕ) (z : ℕ → Eucl d) (hz : ∀ k, z k ∈ sphere d) (R : ℕ → ℝ)
    (hchain : ∀ k, (geodesicBall (z k) (R k) ∩ geodesicBall (z (k + 1)) (R (k + 1))).Nonempty) :
    ∃ θ : Params d, switches θ ≤ K ∧
      (1 - ENNReal.ofReal ε) ^ K * μ (geodesicBall (z 0) (R 0)) ≤
        (measureFlow θ T μ) (geodesicBall (z K) (R K)) := by
  induction K with
  | zero => exact ⟨idParams d, by simp [switches_id], by simp [measureFlow_id]⟩
  | succ k ih => /- compose one lemma_B_2 step onto the chain -/
Statements/MidLevel.lean

title: Blueprint
section: Clustering a single-hemisphere measure to a point (Proposition 2.1)
text:
Clustering a single-hemisphere measure to a point (Proposition 2.1)

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Proposition 2.1, p. 11).
Suppose \(\mathbf B\in\mathcal M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R)\) and \(\operatorname{supp}\mu_0\) is contained in an open hemisphere. Then the solution \(\mu\) to (1.3)-(1.2) with data \(\mu_0\) and \((\mathbf V(\cdot),\mathbf B(\cdot),\mathbf W(\cdot))\equiv(I_d,\mathbf B,0)\) satisfies \(\operatorname{diam}(\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu(t))\to0\) as \(t\to+\infty\). Moreover, for \(\varepsilon>0\) there exist \(z\in\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu_0\) and \(T>0\) such that \[W_\infty(\mu(T),\delta_z)\le\varepsilon\quad\text{and}\quad\inf\{t\ge0:W_2(\mu(t),\delta_z)\le\varepsilon\}=O\Bigl(\log\tfrac1\varepsilon\Bigr).\]
Proof sketch (full proof p. 11; an adaptation of [GLPR25, Lemma 6.4]). The characteristic flow is \(\dot x(t)=\mathbf P_{x(t)}^\perp\mathscr A_{\mathbf B}[\mu(t)](x(t))\). Since \(\operatorname{supp}\mu_0\) lies in an open hemisphere, the normalized attention vector \(\gamma(x):=\mathscr A_{\mathbf B}[\mu_0](x)/\|\mathscr A_{\mathbf B}[\mu_0](x)\|\) is well defined on \(\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu_0\) and points strictly into its interior. A first-order expansion at a boundary point \(x_0\) gives \(\langle x(\tau)-x_0,\gamma(x_0)\rangle>0\) for small \(\tau>0\), so boundary points move inward and the geodesic convex hulls are strictly nested in time, \(\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu(t_2)\subset\operatorname{int}\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu(t_1)\) for \(t_1<t_2\). Hence \(\phi(t):=\operatorname{diam}(\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu(t))\) decreases to some \(\ell\ge0\); \(\ell>0\) would, by compactness, produce boundary points that fail to move inward, contradicting the strict interior-pointing, so \(\ell=0\). Nestedness and vanishing diameter yield a unique \(z\) with \(\mu\rightharpoonup\delta_z\). Picking \(T\) with \(\operatorname{diam}(\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu(T))\le\varepsilon\) and the coupling \((\operatorname{Id},\mathsf T)_{\#}\mu(T)\), \(\mathsf T\equiv z\), gives \(W_p(\mu(T),\delta_z)\le\varepsilon\); the logarithmic rate follows from [CLPR25, Theorem 2.3].


Our formalization. open
The authors’ strategy is a Lyapunov/LaSalle argument on the geodesic convex hull: with the pure self-attention drift (\(\mathbf W=0\), \(\mathbf V=I_d\)) the hull’s diameter is strictly decreasing while its mass stays inside it, so the measure contracts to a single Dirac, the exponential contraction giving the \(O(\log\tfrac1\varepsilon)\) time. We state this type-correctly in Lean — the open hemisphere is supportedIn μ {x | 0 < ⟪e, x⟫} for a unit e, the target a Measure.dirac z, closeness measured in W2 — as an open sorry stub. We do not claim a proof: the diameter monotonicity, the LaSalle/Hartman-Grobman convergence, and the rate live in the continuity-equation flow axiom. The conclusion is validated numerically by experiment E2.
/-- **Proposition 2.1** (clustering to a point). A measure supported in an open hemisphere can be
driven arbitrarily `W₂`-close to a Dirac mass. Stub (`sorry`): `math.open`. The rate
`O(log 1/ε)` and the LaSalle/Hartman-Grobman convergence are in the axiom layer. -/
theorem prop_2_1 (μ : Measure (Eucl d)) (T ε : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T) (hε : 0 < ε)
    (e : Eucl d) (he : ‖e‖ = 1) (hhemi : supportedIn μ {x | 0 < ⟪e, x⟫}) :
    ∃ (θ : Params d) (z : Eucl d), W2 (measureFlow θ T μ) (Measure.dirac z) ≤ ε := by
  sorry

title: Blueprint
text:
Clustering disjoint-support measures to atoms (Proposition 2.2)

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Proposition 2.2, pp. 11-12).
Suppose \(\mu_0^i\) has no atoms and \(\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^i\cap\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^j=\varnothing\) for \(i\ne j\). Fix \(M\ge1\), and for any \(i\in[\![1,N]\!]\) consider \[\mu_1^i:=\sum_{k=1}^M\alpha_k^i\,\delta_{x_k^i}\in\mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\] where \(x_k^i\in\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^i\), with \(x_k^i=x_{k'}^j\) if and only if \((k,i)=(k',j)\). Then for any \(T>0\) and \(\varepsilon>0\) there exist piecewise constant \((\mathbf W,\mathbf U,b):[0,T]\to\mathcal M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R)^2\times\mathbb R^d\) such that for any \(i\), the corresponding solution \(\mu^i\) to (1.3)-(1.2) with data \(\mu_0^i\), \(\mathbf V\equiv0\) and the above parameters, satisfies \(W_2(\mu^i(T),\mu_1^i)\le\varepsilon\) as well as, for \(i\ne j\), \[\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu^i(T)\cap\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu^j(T)=\varnothing.\] The number of switches in \((\mathbf W,\mathbf V,b)\) can also be accounted for — see Remark 2.3.
Proof sketch (full proof from p. 12). Split \([0,T]=\bigcup_{i\in[\![1,N]\!]}[T_{i-1},T_i]\) and apply Lemma B.1 separately within each interval, acting on one measure at a time. Property (B.2) — the flow is the identity off the active balls — ensures that while the construction acts on the \(i\)-th measure the others are untouched, \(\mu^i(T_{i-1})=\mu_0^i\). title: Blueprint
text:
Transport to the orthant

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Lemma 3.2, p. 15).
Suppose \(T>0\) and \(\mu_0^i\in\mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\) with \(\bigcup_i\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^i\subsetneq\mathbb S^{d-1}\). There exists a piecewise constant \(\mathbf W:[0,T]\to\mathscr M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R)\), having at most one switch and satisfying \[\|\mathbf W\|_{L^\infty((0,T);\mathscr M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R))}\le C/T\] for some \(C=C(N)>0\), such that for any \(i\in[\![1,N]\!]\) the solution \(\mu^i\) to (1.3)–(1.2) with data \(\mu_0^i\) and \(\mathbf V\equiv\mathbf B\equiv\mathbf U\equiv 0\), \(b\equiv\mathbf 1\), satisfies \(\operatorname{supp}\mu^i(T)\subset\mathbb Q_1^{d-1}\).
Proof. (proof sketch; full proof p. 15.) Pick a direction \(\omega\in\mathbb S^{d-1}\setminus\bigcup_i\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^i\) not filling the sphere and set \(\mathbf W_1\mathbf 1=-\omega\). The characteristics solve \(\dot x=-\mathbf P_x^\perp\omega\), so \(\frac{d}{dt}\langle x,\omega\rangle=-1+\langle x,\omega\rangle^2<0\) off \(\{\pm\omega\}\), hence by some \(T_0\) all supports lie in \(B(-\omega,\pi/8)\). Pick \(\alpha\in\mathbb Q_1^{d-1}\) with \(d_g(-\omega,-\alpha)>\pi/8\) (so \(-\alpha\) is off every support) and on \([T_0,T]\) take \(\mathbf W_2\mathbf 1=\alpha\): now \(\frac{d}{dt}\langle x,\alpha\rangle=1-\langle x,\alpha\rangle^2\ge0\) drives every point into a prescribed small cap around \(\alpha\in\mathbb Q_1^{d-1}\). One switch; the norm bound follows by time-rescaling the two phases. \(\square\)


Our formalization. open
The authors’ construction (attributed to them) is the one switch above: a first phase \(\mathbf W_1\mathbf 1=-\omega\) contracts every support toward \(-\omega\) through the gate-free dynamics \(\frac{d}{dt}\langle x,\omega\rangle=-(1-\langle x,\omega\rangle^2)\) — the projector identity of leaf L1 — and a second phase \(\mathbf W_2\mathbf 1=\alpha\in\mathbb Q_1^{d-1}\) drives them into a small cap inside the orthant. We give a type-correct Lean statement against the axiom layer (measureFlow, switches), expressing “support inside \(\mathbb Q_1^{d-1}\)” measure-theoretically as μ (orthant d)ᶜ = 0. It is a sorry stub: we state it, we do not claim a proof; its discharge rests on the continuity-equation flow axiom.
/-- The open positive orthant `Q₁^{d-1} = 𝕊^{d-1} ∩ (ℝ_{>0})^d`, as a subset of `ℝ^d`. -/
def orthant (d : ℕ) : Set (Eucl d) := {x | ∀ i, 0 < x i}

/-- "The support of `μ` is contained in `S`", expressed measure-theoretically as `μ(Sᶜ) = 0`. -/
def supportedIn (μ : Measure (Eucl d)) (S : Set (Eucl d)) : Prop := μ Sᶜ = 0

`Q₁^{d-1}`. Stub (`sorry`): `math.open`. -/

text:
Shrinking a hull to its barycenter direction

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Lemma 3.3, p. 16; proof App. B.2, p. 33).
Let \(\mu_0^i\in\mathscr P(\mathbb Q_1^{d-1})\) be such that \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^i}[x]\) is not colinear with \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^j}[x]\) for \(i\ne j\). Then for any \(T>0\), \(\varepsilon>0\), and \(\nu_0\in\mathscr P(\mathbb Q_1^{d-1})\) such that \(\mathbb E_{\nu_0}[x]\) is colinear with \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^i}[x]\), there exists a piecewise constant \(\theta:[0,T]\to\Theta\) having at most \(O(d\cdot N)\) switches such that \[\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\nu(T)\cup\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu^N(T)\subset B\!\left(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^j}[z]\,\Big/\,\big\|\mathbb E_{\mu_0^j}[z]\big\|,\ \varepsilon\right)\] and \(\mu^i(T)=\mu_0^i\) for \(i\ne j\), where \(\mu^i,\nu\) denote the unique solutions to (1.3)–(3.1) corresponding to data \(\mu_0^i,\nu_0\), and parameters \(\theta\).
Proof. (proof sketch; full proof App. B.2, p. 33.) Three steps. (1) Isolating \(\mu_0^N,\nu_0\): with \(\mathbf W\equiv 0\) and \(\mathbf V(t)=\sum_k\alpha_k\alpha_k^\top\mathbf 1_{[T_{k-1},T_k]}\), where \(\{\alpha_k\}\) is an orthonormal basis of \(\operatorname{span}(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^N}[z])^\perp\), the barycenter ODE (B.9) \(\frac{d}{dt}\langle x,\alpha_1\rangle=\langle\mathbb E_{\mu(t)}[x],\alpha_1\rangle(1-\langle\alpha_1,x\rangle^2)\) sends \(x(t)\to\pm\alpha_k\), concentrating \(\operatorname{supp}\mu(T_{d-1})\) near \(\bigcup_k B(\pm\alpha_k,C_k\varepsilon_k)\subset\mathbb S^{d-1}\setminus\mathbb Q_1^{d-1}\) (flow \(\Psi_1\), fixing \(\mu_0^j\) for \(j\le N-1\)). (2) Clustering: a separating half-space \(\langle a,x\rangle+\underline b\) with \(\mathbf U=\mathbf 1a^\top\), \(\mathbf W_2\mathbf 1=\mathbb E_{\mu_0^N}[x]\) drives both supports into \(B(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^N}[x]/\|\mathbb E_{\mu_0^N}[x]\|,\delta)\) (flow \(\Psi_2\)). (3) Flow reversal: \(\Phi_{\mathrm{fin}}:=\Psi_1^{-1}\circ\Psi_2\circ\Psi_1\) returns \(\mu_0^i\) to itself for \(i\le N-1\) and clusters \(\nu,\mu^N\) within \(B(\,\cdot\,,C_T\delta)\); pick \(\delta\) small. \(\square\)


Our formalization. open
The authors’ three-step argument (App. B.2) hinges on the barycenter ODE (B.9), which is exactly our machine-checked leaf L6 (barycenter_hasDerivAt_inner): along the attention field, \(\frac{d}{dt}\langle x,\alpha\rangle = c\,(1-\langle x,\alpha\rangle^2)\) with strict increase for \(c>0\), which is what drives each point to \(\pm\alpha\) and lets the support be concentrated into an \(\varepsilon\)-ball around the barycenter direction. We state the conclusion type-correctly against the axiom layer: some \(\theta\) concentrates the flow into Metric.ball α ε. It is a sorry stub stated against measureFlow; the analytic content (the three flows \(\Psi_1,\Psi_2,\Psi_1^{-1}\)) lives in the flow axiom.

measure can be concentrated into a small ball around some direction `α`, with `O(dN)` switches.
Stub (`sorry`): `math.open`. -/

title: Blueprint
text:
Disentanglement

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Proposition 3.1, pp. 15-17).
Let \(T>0\) and \(\mu_0^i\in\mathscr P(\mathbb Q_1^{d-1})\). There exists a piecewise constant \(\theta:[0,T]\to\Theta\), having at most \(O(d\cdot N)\) switches, such that for all \(i\in[\![1,N]\!]\), the solution \(\mu^i\) to (1.3)–(3.1) with data \(\mu_0^i\) and \(\theta\) satisfies \[\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu^i(T)\cap\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu^j(T)=\varnothing\quad\text{if } i\ne j.\]
Proof. (proof sketch; full proof pp. 16-17.) Induction on \(N\); the base case \(N=1\) is trivial. Assume the first \(N-1\) measures already have pairwise-disjoint geodesic hulls (3.3) and let \(\mu_0^N\) be arbitrary. Since \(\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^i\subset\mathbb Q_1^{d-1}\), (3.3) implies \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^i}[x]\) is not colinear with \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^j}[x]\) for \(i\ne j\le N-1\). If \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^N}[x]\) is non-colinear with all of them, one application of Lemma 3.3 (small \(\varepsilon\)) shrinks \(\mu^N\)’s hull until it separates. Otherwise \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^N}[x]\) is colinear with \(\mathbb E_{\mu_0^i}[x]\) for at most one \(i\) (WLOG \(i=N-1\)), and four phases of \([0,T]\) finish it: (1) \([0,T/4]\) Lemma 3.3 peels \(\mu^N,\mu^{N-1}\) off the rest; (2) \([T/4,T/2]\) Lemma 3.4 Part 1 makes \(\mathbb E_{\mu^{N-1}}[x]\ne\mathbb E_{\mu^N}[x]\) while (3.2) keeps the others fixed; (3) \([T/2,3T/4]\) Lemma 3.4 Part 2 makes the two barycenters non-colinear; (4) \([3T/4,T]\) Lemma 3.3, now applicable to all \(N\) measures, separates them. Total \(O(d\cdot N)\) switches. \(\square\)


Our formalization. open
We state the headline controllability conclusion of the authors’ induction type-correctly against the axiom layer: there is a \(\theta\) whose solution map renders the family’s supports pairwise disjoint, recorded by the opaque predicate DisjointSupports. It is a sorry stub — the four-phase induction, the \(O(dN)\) switch count, and the geodesic-hull nesting all rest on the flow / \(\operatorname{conv}_g\) axioms, so we do not claim a proof.
/-- The measures in the family have pairwise disjoint (geodesic-convex-hull) supports. -/
axiom DisjointSupports {N : ℕ} (ν : Fin N → Measure (Eucl d)) : Prop

/-- **Proposition 3.1** (disentanglement). There is a piecewise-constant `θ` whose solution map
renders the supports of the family pairwise disjoint. Stub (`sorry`): status `math.open`. -/

    ∃ θ : Params d, DisjointSupports (fun i => measureFlow θ T (μ₀ i)) := by
  sorry

What we strengthen. (finding F2.) The induction above opens by asserting that disjoint geodesic hulls imply non-colinear barycenters — \[\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^i\cap\operatorname{conv}_g\operatorname{supp}\mu_0^j=\varnothing\ \Longrightarrow\ \mathbb E_{\mu_0^i}[x]\ \text{not colinear with}\ \mathbb E_{\mu_0^j}[x]\] — without proof. Our adversarial review flagged this as the one genuine rigor gap (finding F2). It is now the machine-checked leaf L11 (barycenter_noncolinear_of_disjoint_hull, see the Self-contained leaves section) for the empirical regime of Theorem 1.1 / restricted Theorem 1.2, and L11\('\) (barycenter_noncolinear_of_disjoint_hull_general) for arbitrary probability measures via Mathlib’s Convex.integral_mem. The barycenter, a nonnegative average of support points, lies in \(\operatorname{cone}(\operatorname{supp})\); colinearity would put a common normalized direction in both hulls, contradicting disjointness. The headline prop_3_1 stays [open]{.badge .open} only because it still rests on the flow / \(\operatorname{conv}_g\)-nesting axioms — not on this step. (Validated numerically by experiment E3.)

title: Blueprint
text:
Proposition 4.2 — steer the active point

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, §4, Proposition 4.2, p. 18).
Suppose \(d \geqslant 3\). Consider \[(x_0^i, y^i) \in \mathbb S^{d-1} \times \mathbb S^{d-1} \qquad \text{for } i \in [\![1, M]\!], \tag{$\mathscr D$}\] with \(x_0^i \neq x_0^j\) and \(y^i \neq y^j\) for \(i \neq j\), with \(x_0^i = y^i\) for \(i \in [\![1, M-1]\!]\), and suppose that there exist \(\gamma \in \mathbb S^{d-1}\) and \(\varepsilon > 0\) such that \(\langle \gamma, x_0^M - y^M \rangle = 0\) and \(x_0^i \notin H_\varepsilon^\gamma\) for all \(i \in [\![1, M-1]\!]\), where \(H_\varepsilon^\gamma := \{x \in \mathbb S^{d-1} : |\langle x, \gamma\rangle| \leqslant \varepsilon\}\).
For any \(T > 0\) there exist piecewise constant \(\theta = (\mathbf W, \mathbf U, b) : [0, T] \to \mathcal M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R)^2 \times \mathbb R^d\), having at most \(6\) switches, such that for any \(i\), the solution \(x^i(\cdot)\) to \[\begin{cases}\dot x^i(t) = \mathbf P_x^\perp\,\mathbf W(t)\big(\mathbf U(t)x^i(t) + b(t)\big)_+ & \text{in } [0,T]\\ x^i(0) = x_0^i,\end{cases} \tag{4.1}\] satisfies \(x^i(T) = y^i\). Moreover, there exists \(C > 0\), not depending on \(\mathscr D\) and \(T\), such that \[\|\theta\|_{L^\infty((0,T);\Theta)} \leqslant \frac{C}{T\cdot\varepsilon}.\]
Proof sketch (proof sketch; full proof pp. 18-23). The control is a \(6\)-interval step function \((\mathbf W(t),\mathbf U(t),b(t)) = \sum_{j=1}^6 (\mathbf W_j,\mathbf U_j,b_j)\,\mathbf 1_{[(j-1)T/6,\,jT/6]}(t)\) with \(\mathbf U_5=\mathbf U_1,\ \mathbf U_6=\mathbf U_2,\ \mathbf U_3=\mathbf U_4,\ b_5=b_1,\ b_6=b_2,\ b_3=b_4,\ \mathbf W_5=-\mathbf W_1,\ \mathbf W_6=-\mathbf W_2\), i.e. the last two intervals time-reverse the first two — at most \(6\) switches. Anchors (Step 1). Since \(\langle\gamma,x_0^M-y^M\rangle=0\) one finds \(\omega\in\mathbb S^{d-1}\) with \(\langle\gamma,\omega\rangle=0\) and \(d_g(\omega,x_0^M),d_g(\omega,y^M)\geqslant\pi/2\); the points \(\omega_\pm\) at geodesic distance \(\pi/8\) from \(\omega\) toward \(\pm\gamma\) then satisfy \(d_g(\omega_\pm,x_0^M),d_g(\omega_\pm,y^M)\geqslant 3\pi/8\), so \(\{\langle\omega,x\rangle=\cos(\pi/8+\tau)\}\) separates the cap \(B(\omega,\pi/8+\tau)\) from \(x_0^M,y^M\). Phase 1 — “gather” \(\psi_1\) (Steps 1-2, \(2\) switches). Two opposite gates \((\langle\pm\gamma,x\rangle-\epsilon/2)_+\) with drift targets \(\omega_\pm\) run the Riemannian gradient flow of \(d_g(\cdot,\omega_\pm)\) (using \(\nabla_1 d_g(x,\omega_+)=-\mathbf P_x^\perp\omega_+/\sqrt{1-\langle x,\omega_+\rangle^2}\)); \(\langle x,\omega_\pm\rangle\) increases monotonically (4.5) and the trajectory settles exponentially near \(\omega_\pm\) by LaSalle / Hartman-Grobman (4.6), so after time \(T/3\) every inactive point lies in the cap \(B(\omega,3\pi/16)\) while points of \(H_\varepsilon^\gamma\) stay fixed (4.7). Phase 2 — “corridor” \(\psi_2\) (Step 3, \(2\) switches). The gate \((\langle-\omega,x\rangle+\cos(3\pi/16))_+\) is identically \(0\) on \(B(\omega,3\pi/16)\) and positive outside; driving \(x_0^M\) first toward an intermediate \(z_1\) then toward \(z_2\) along a geodesic corridor that lies outside the cap and passes through \(y^M\) lands the active point at \(y^M\), while the inactive points do not move (gate off on the cap). Phase 3 — “restore” \(\psi_1^{-1}\) (Step 4, \(2\) switches). Re-running the gather action with flipped drifts time-reverses Phase 1, returning every inactive point to its start; the active point remains at \(y^M\) since the corridor gate is off on the cap. The composition \((\psi_1)^{-1}\circ\psi_2\circ\psi_1\) sends \(x_0^M\mapsto y^M\) and fixes all other \(x_0^i\); choosing drift size \(\|\mathbf W\|\asymp(T\varepsilon)^{-1}\) gives total time \(T\) and \(\|\theta\|_{L^\infty}\lesssim 1/(T\varepsilon)\).


Our formalization. open
The authors’ construction (pp. 18-23) realises the matching of one point as the gather / corridor / restore composition \((\psi_1)^{-1}\circ\psi_2\circ\psi_1\), a \(6\)-interval piecewise-constant control whose middle phase steers the active \(x_0^M\) to \(y^M\) while a gate parked on the cap \(B(\omega,3\pi/16)\) freezes the inactive points. We record a type-correct Lean statement of this proposition as a sorry (open): with \(d\geqslant 3\), the inactive points already at their targets (hfix), at most \(6\) switches drive every input to its target while keeping the inactive ones fixed. We do not claim a proof. Two of the construction’s cores are however discharged as kernel-checked leaves: the separating-hyperplane estimate of Step 1 is leaf L3 (Leaves/SeparatingHyperplane.lean, separating_hyperplane), and the geodesic-gradient identity driving the gather and corridor flows is leaf L4 (Leaves/GeodesicGradient.lean, geodesicDist_hasDerivAt). The selective-motion mechanism — active point reaches the target, parked points stay fixed — is validated numerically by experiment E4.
/-- **Proposition 4.2** (steer one active point). With `d ≥ 3` and the inactive points already at
their targets, at most `6` switches move every input to its target, keeping the inactive ones fixed
(`x₀ i = y i` is preserved by `flowMap θ T (x₀ i) = y i`). Stub (`sorry`): `math.open`. Step 1 is
leaf L3, the geodesic gradient is leaf L4. -/
theorem prop_4_2 (hd : 3 ≤ d) (M : ℕ) (x₀ y : Fin M → Eucl d) (T : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T)
    (hfix : ∀ i : Fin M, (i : ℕ) < M - 1 → x₀ i = y i) :
    ∃ θ : Params d, switches θ ≤ 6 ∧ ∀ i, flowMap θ T (x₀ i) = y i := by
  sorry

text:
Proposition 4.1 — match the ensemble

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, §4, Proposition 4.1, p. 18).
Suppose \(d \geqslant 3\). Consider \[(x_0^i, y^i) \in \mathbb S^{d-1} \times \mathbb S^{d-1} \qquad \text{for } i \in [\![1, M]\!], \tag{$\mathscr D$}\] with \(x_0^i \neq x_0^j\) and \(y^i \neq y^j\) for \(i \neq j\), and suppose that for any \(i\) there exist \(\gamma_i \in \mathbb S^{d-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_i > 0\) such that \(\langle \gamma_i, x_0^i - y^i\rangle = 0\) and \(x_0^j \notin H_{\varepsilon_i}^{\gamma_i}\) for \(j \neq i\), where \[H_{\varepsilon_i}^{\gamma_i} := \{x \in \mathbb S^{d-1} : |\langle x, \gamma_i\rangle| \leqslant \varepsilon_i\}.\]
For any \(T > 0\) there exist piecewise constant \(\theta = (\mathbf W, \mathbf U, b) : [0, T] \to \mathcal M_{d\times d}(\mathbb R)^2 \times \mathbb R^d\), having at most \(6M\) switches, such that for any \(i\), the solution \(x^i(\cdot) \in \mathscr C^0([0,T];\mathbb S^{d-1})\) to (4.1) satisfies \(x^i(T) = y^i\). Moreover, there exists \(C > 0\), not depending on \(\mathscr D\) nor \(T\), such that \[\|\theta\|_{L^\infty((0,T);\Theta)} \leqslant \frac{C\cdot M}{T\,\min_i \varepsilon_i}.\]
Proof sketch (proof sketch; full proof p. 18). “The proof of Proposition 4.1 follows directly from [Proposition 4.2], combined with a straightforward induction argument”: activating one pair \((x_0^i,y^i)\) at a time, each application of Proposition 4.2 steers the \(i\)-th point to its target in at most \(6\) switches while leaving the already-matched points fixed, so \(M\) applications cost at most \(6M\) switches and the norm bound accumulates to \(C\cdot M / (T\min_i\varepsilon_i)\).


Our formalization. open
The authors obtain Proposition 4.1 by induction over the \(M\) pairs, each step being one application of Proposition 4.2 (the active point reaches its target; the previously matched points are inactive and stay fixed), giving the \(\leqslant 6M\)-switch budget and the \(O\!\big(CM/(T\min_i\varepsilon_i)\big)\) norm. We record a type-correct Lean statement as a sorry (open): with \(d\geqslant 3\) and injective inputs/targets, at most \(6M\) switches steer every \(x_0^i\) to \(y^i\). We do not claim a proof; the discharge would be the \(M\)-fold composition of prop_4_2. (Validated numerically by experiment E4, alongside Proposition 4.2.)
/-- **Proposition 4.1** (match an ensemble). With `d ≥ 3` and distinct inputs/targets, at most `6M`
switches steer every `x₀ i` to `y i`. Stub (`sorry`): `math.open`. Follows from Proposition 4.2 by
induction. -/
theorem prop_4_1 (hd : 3 ≤ d) (M : ℕ) (x₀ y : Fin M → Eucl d) (T : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T)
    (hx₀ : Function.Injective x₀) (hy : Function.Injective y) :
    ∃ θ : Params d, switches θ ≤ 6 * M ∧ ∀ i, flowMap θ T (x₀ i) = y i := by
  sorry
Statements/MidLevel.lean

title: Blueprint
section: Lemma 5.1 — transport maps preserved by disentanglement
text:
Lemma 5.1 — transport maps preserved by disentanglement

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Lemma 5.1, p. 24).
Suppose that for every \(i\) there exists \(\mathsf T^i \in L^2(\mathbb S^{d-1};\mathbb S^{d-1})\) with \(\mathsf T^i_\#\mu_0^i = \mu_1^i\). Consider the flow map \(\Phi_1 : \mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1}) \to \mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\) (resp. \(\Phi_3\)) given by Proposition 3.1 with data \(\mu_0^i \in \mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\) (resp. \(\mu_1^i \in \mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\)). Then there exists a Lipschitz-continuous and invertible map \(\psi : \mathbb S^{d-1} \to \mathbb S^{d-1}\) such that \[\psi\big|_{\operatorname{supp}\Phi_1(\mu_0^i)} = \psi^i\big|_{\operatorname{supp}\Phi_1(\mu_0^i)} \tag{5.1}\] for any \(i \in \{1,\dots,N\}\), where \(\psi^i : \mathbb S^{d-1} \to \mathbb S^{d-1}\) is another Lipschitz-continuous and invertible map that satisfies \[(\psi^i \circ \mathsf T^i_{\Phi_1})_\#\mu_0^i = (\psi \circ \mathsf T^i_{\Phi_1})_\#\mu_0^i = (\mathsf T^i_{\Phi_3})_\#\mu_1^i = \Phi_3(\mu_1^i)\] for some Lipschitz-continuous and invertible maps \(\mathsf T^i_{\Phi_1}, \mathsf T^i_{\Phi_3} : \mathbb S^{d-1} \to \mathbb S^{d-1}\). The proof can be found in Appendix B.4.


Our formalization. open
The paper’s strategy (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, App. B.4, p. 37) exploits disentanglement: once Proposition 3.1 has pushed the supports \(\operatorname{supp}\Phi_1(\mu_0^i)\) pairwise disjoint, the per-pair transport maps \(\mathsf T^i\) are carried through the flow as Lipschitz-invertible maps \(\mathsf T^i_{\Phi_1}, \mathsf T^i_{\Phi_3}\), and the individual matchings \(\psi^i\) can be glued into one global Lipschitz-continuous, invertible \(\psi\) that agrees with each \(\psi^i\) on the \(i\)-th (now disjoint) support and realizes \((\psi\circ \mathsf T^i_{\Phi_1})_\#\mu_0^i = \Phi_3(\mu_1^i)\). We give a type-correct Lean statement as a sorry (open), abstracting the Lipschitz/geodesic bookkeeping into the matchability hypothesis: from a per-index family of transport maps we ask for a single Bijective \(\psi\) pushing every \(\mu_0^i\) to \(\mu_1^i\). We do not claim a proof.
/-- **Lemma 5.1** (transport map after disentanglement). If each disentangled pair is matchable, a
single bijective map matches them all. Stub (`sorry`): `math.open`. -/
theorem lemma_5_1 {N : ℕ} (μ₀ μ₁ : Fin N → Measure (Eucl d))
    (hmatch : ∀ i, ∃ Ti : Eucl d → Eucl d, (μ₀ i).map Ti = μ₁ i) :
    ∃ ψ : Eucl d → Eucl d, Function.Bijective ψ ∧ ∀ i, (μ₀ i).map ψ = μ₁ i := by
  sorry
Statements/MidLevel.lean

title: Blueprint
section: Lemma 5.4 — L² approximation by a flow map
text:
Lemma 5.4 — L² approximation by a flow map

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Lemma 5.4, p. 24).
Suppose \(\varepsilon > 0\) and \(\mu \in \mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1})\). For every \(\psi \in L^2(\mathbb S^{d-1};\mathbb S^{d-1})\), there exists a Lipschitz-continuous and invertible map \(\psi_\varepsilon : \mathbb S^{d-1} \to \mathbb S^{d-1}\) induced by the solution map of (B.1), namely \(\Phi^T_{\theta_\varepsilon}(\mu) = (\psi_\varepsilon)_\#\mu\) for some piecewise constant \(\theta_\varepsilon : [0,T] \to \Theta\) with finitely many switches, such that \[\|\psi - \psi_\varepsilon\|_{L^2(\mu)} \leq \varepsilon.\] The proof of Lemma 5.4 is involved, so we postpone it to Appendix B.5.


Our formalization. open
The paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, App. B.5, p. 38) shows any \(\psi \in L^2(\mathbb S^{d-1};\mathbb S^{d-1})\) is approximated in \(L^2(\mu)\), to any tolerance, by a flow map \(\psi_\varepsilon\) of the continuity equation (B.1) with piecewise-constant control and finitely many switches; chained with the coupling bound \(W_2(\mathsf T^1_\#\mu, \mathsf T^2_\#\mu) \le \|\mathsf T^1-\mathsf T^2\|_{L^2(\mu)}\) (Lemma 5.2, leaf L7) this converts \(L^2\) control of the map into \(W_2\) control of the pushforward. We give a type-correct Lean statement as a sorry (open): for any \(\psi\) and \(\varepsilon\), a parameter \(\theta\) and a map \(\psi_\varepsilon\) with measureFlow θ T μ = μ.map ψε and \(\sqrt{\int \|\psi-\psi_\varepsilon\|^2\,\mathrm d\mu} \le \varepsilon\). We do not claim a proof.
/-- **Lemma 5.4** (`L²` approximation by a flow map). Any transport map `ψ` is approximated in
`L²(μ)` by a flow map of the dynamics, to any tolerance, with finitely many switches. Stub
(`sorry`): `math.open`. Combined with the coupling bound (leaf L7) this controls `W₂`. -/
theorem lemma_5_4 (μ : Measure (Eucl d)) (ψ : Eucl d → Eucl d) (T ε : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T) (hε : 0 < ε) :
    ∃ (θ : Params d) (ψε : Eucl d → Eucl d),
      measureFlow θ T μ = μ.map ψε ∧ Real.sqrt (∫ x, ‖ψ x - ψε x‖ ^ 2 ∂μ) ≤ ε := by
  sorry
Statements/MidLevel.lean

title: Blueprint
text:
Theorem 1.2 — matching transport-matchable targets

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Theorem 1.2, p. 5).
Suppose \(d \geq 3\). Consider data \((\mathscr D)\) such that

there exist \(w_0, w_1 \in \mathbb S^{d-1}\) such that \[w_0 \notin \bigcup_i \operatorname{supp}(\mu_0^i) \quad\text{and}\quad w_1 \notin \bigcup_i \operatorname{supp}(\mu_1^i); \tag{1.5}\]
for any \(i \in \{1,\dots,N\}\), there exists \(\mathsf T^i \in L^2(\mathbb S^{d-1};\mathbb S^{d-1})\) such that \(\mathsf T^i_\#\mu_0^i = \mu_1^i\).

Then for any \(T > 0\) and \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists \(\theta \in L^\infty((0,T);\Theta)\) such that for any \(i \in \{1,\dots,N\}\), the unique solution \(\mu^i \in \mathscr C^0([0,T];\mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1}))\) to (1.3) with data \(\mu_0^i\) and parameters \(\theta\) satisfies \[\mathsf W_2(\mu^i(T), \mu_1^i) \leq \varepsilon.\] Moreover, \(\theta\) can be chosen piecewise constant.
Proof sketch (full proof §5.1, p. 25). The construction \(\Phi^T_{\mathrm{fin}} := (\Phi_{\theta_3}^{T/3})^{-1} \circ \Phi_{\theta_2}^{T/3} \circ \Phi_{\theta_1}^{T/3}\) proceeds in three steps. (1) Disentanglement: Proposition 3.1 renders the input supports \((\mu_0^i)\) pairwise disjoint via \(\Phi_{\theta_1}\) on \([0,T/3]\) and the target supports \((\mu_1^i)\) via \(\Phi_{\theta_3}\) on \([2T/3,T]\); well-posedness and time-reversibility give a constant \(C\) with \(\mathsf W_2((\Phi_{\theta_3}^T)^{-1}\mu,(\Phi_{\theta_3}^T)^{-1}\nu) \le C\,\mathsf W_2(\mu,\nu)\) (5.3). (2) Matching: Lemma 5.1 produces a single Lipschitz-invertible \(\psi\) agreeing with each \(\psi^i\) on the disentangled support, and Lemma 5.4 realizes \(\psi\) up to \(L^2\) error by a flow map \(\psi_\varepsilon = \Phi_{\theta_2}\) on \([T/3,2T/3]\); the coupling bound (Lemma 5.2) turns the \(L^2\) error into \(\mathsf W_2\big((\Phi_{\theta_2}^{2T/3}\!\circ\Phi_{\theta_1}^{T/3})(\mu_0^i),\Phi_{\theta_3}^T(\mu_1^i)\big) \le \varepsilon/C\) (5.5–5.6). (3) Composition: applying \((\Phi_{\theta_3}^T)^{-1}\) and (5.3) yields \(\mathsf W_2(\mu^i(T),\mu_1^i) \le \varepsilon\) for all \(i\).


Our formalization. open
The paper’s proof (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, §5.1) composes the three stages above: disentangle both families by Proposition 3.1, transfer the transport maps into a single \(\psi\) by Lemma 5.1, approximate \(\psi\) by a flow map by Lemma 5.4, and bound the resulting \(W_2\) error by the coupling Lemma 5.2. We give a type-correct Lean statement as a sorry (open). The hypothesis (1.5) — a shared missing direction off every input support and off every target support — and the \(L^2\)-matchability of the pairs are recorded as the opaque predicates SharedMissingDirection and Matchable, so the statement stays faithful without re-deriving the absent support / optimal-transport API; the conclusion is the controllability core, a single piecewise-constant \(\theta\) with W2 (measureFlow θ T (μ₀ i)) (μ₁ i) ≤ ε for every \(i\). We do not claim a proof.
/-- **Theorem 1.2** (general targets). If every input/target pair is matchable by a transport map
and the families share a missing direction, then for any horizon `T` and tolerance `ε` there is a
piecewise-constant parameter `θ` whose solution map steers each input measure to within `ε` of its
target in `W₂`. Stub (`sorry`): status `math.open`. -/
theorem theorem_1_2 (hd : 3 ≤ d) {N : ℕ} (μ₀ μ₁ : Fin N → Measure (Eucl d))
    (T ε : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T) (hε : 0 < ε)
    (hmiss₀ : SharedMissingDirection μ₀) (hmiss₁ : SharedMissingDirection μ₁)
    (hmatch : Matchable μ₀ μ₁) :
    ∃ θ : Params d, ∀ i, W2 (measureFlow θ T (μ₀ i)) (μ₁ i) ≤ ε := by
  sorry
Statements/MainResults.lean

title: Blueprint
text:
Theorem 1.1 — matching Dirac targets

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Theorem 1.1, p. 5).
Suppose \(d \geq 3\). Consider data \((\mathscr D)\) such that

there exists \(w_0 \in \mathbb S^{d-1}\) such that \[w_0 \notin \bigcup_i \operatorname{supp}(\mu_0^i); \tag{1.4}\]
for any \(i \in \{1,\dots,N\}\), we have \(\mu_1^i = \delta_{x^i}\).

Then for any \(T > 0\) and \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists \(\theta \in L^\infty((0,T);\Theta)\) such that for any \(i \in \{1,\dots,N\}\), the unique solution \(\mu^i \in \mathscr C^0([0,T];\mathscr P(\mathbb S^{d-1}))\) to (1.3) with data \(\mu_0^i\) and parameters \(\theta\) satisfies \[\mathsf W_2(\mu^i(T), \mu_1^i) \leq \varepsilon.\] Moreover, \(\theta\) can be chosen piecewise constant, with \(O(d\cdot N)\) switches, and \[\|\theta\|_{L^\infty((0,T);\Theta)} = O\!\left(\frac{d\cdot N}{T} + \log\frac1\varepsilon\right).\]
Proof sketch (full proof §5.2, p. 29). The proof follows the same ideas as Theorem 1.2 but is significantly simpler — some steps are omitted — using \(\Phi^T_{\mathrm{fin}} := \Phi_{\theta_3}^{T} \circ \Phi_{\theta_2}^{2T/3} \circ \Phi_{\theta_1}^{T/3}\). (1) Disentangle: \(\Phi_{\theta_1}\) on \([0,T/3]\) from Proposition 3.1, with \(\|\theta_1\| \lesssim d\,N/T\). (2) Cluster: \(\Phi_{\theta_2}\) on \([T/3,2T/3]\) from Proposition 2.1, applicable since the \(\Phi^{T/3}(\mu_0^i)\) are now disjoint and single-hemisphere, driving each to within \(\delta\) of a point \(\delta_{x_0^i}\) with \(T_\delta = O(\log 1/\delta)\), so \(\|\theta_2\| \lesssim \log 1/\delta\). (3) Match: \(\Phi_{\theta_3}\) on \([2T/3,T]\) from Proposition 4.1 steers the ensemble of atoms \(x_0^i\) to the targets \(y^i\), with \(\|\theta_3\| \lesssim N/T\); since all measures are \(\delta\)-close to \(\delta_{x_0^i}\) the error is \(e^{O(N\cdot T)}\delta\). All in all \(\mathsf W_2(\mu^i(T),\delta_{y^i}) \le \varepsilon\) with \(\|\theta\|_{L^\infty((0,T);\Theta)} = O(d\cdot N/T + \log 1/\varepsilon)\).


Our formalization. open
The paper’s proof (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, §5.2) is the simpler Dirac-target specialization: disentangle by Proposition 3.1 (\(\|\theta_1\| \lesssim dN/T\)), cluster each now-disjoint single-hemisphere measure to within \(\delta\) of a point by Proposition 2.1 (\(T_\delta = O(\log 1/\delta)\), \(\|\theta_2\| \lesssim \log 1/\delta\)), then match the ensemble of cluster atoms \(x_0^i\) to the targets \(y^i\) by Proposition 4.1 (\(\|\theta_3\| \lesssim N/T\)); Lemma 5.4 is not needed. The end-to-end construction is validated numerically by experiment E6, while the negative control E7 shows that a single linear continuity equation cannot achieve it — motivating the measure-dependent vector field. We give a type-correct Lean statement as a sorry (open): a shared missing direction and Dirac targets \(\delta_{x^i}\) yield a single piecewise-constant \(\theta\) with W2 (measureFlow θ T (μ₀ i)) (Measure.dirac (x i)) ≤ ε for every \(i\). The \(O(d\,N)\) switch count and the norm bound \(O(dN/T + \log 1/\varepsilon)\) are documented in this blueprint prose, not encoded in the Lean type. We do not claim a proof.
/-- **Theorem 1.1** (Dirac targets). If the targets are point masses `δ_{x i}` and the inputs share
a missing direction, then for any horizon and tolerance a single piecewise-constant `θ` steers each
input to within `ε` of its target in `W₂`. Stub (`sorry`): status `math.open`. The proof also gives
`O(d N)` switches and the stated norm bound (see the blueprint). -/
theorem theorem_1_1 (hd : 3 ≤ d) {N : ℕ} (μ₀ : Fin N → Measure (Eucl d)) (x : Fin N → Eucl d)
    (T ε : ℝ) (hT : 0 < T) (hε : 0 < ε) (hmiss : SharedMissingDirection μ₀) :
    ∃ θ : Params d, ∀ i, W2 (measureFlow θ T (μ₀ i)) (Measure.dirac (x i)) ≤ ε := by
  sorry
Statements/MainResults.lean

title: Blueprint
text:
Proposition 6.2 — generic O(1) disentanglement

From the original paper (Geshkovski, Rigollet, Ruiz-Balet, Proposition 6.2, p. 30).
Let \(d \geq 3\), \(\pi = \operatorname{Unif}((\mathbb S^{d-1})^n)\), and sample \(\mu_0^1, \dots, \mu_0^N\) i.i.d. from \[\mathscr S_n := \left\{ \frac1n \sum_{k=1}^n \delta_{x_k} : x_k \in \mathbb S^{d-1} \right\} \simeq (\mathbb S^{d-1})^n.\] Consider (1.3)–(1.2) with \(\mathbf B \equiv \beta I_d\) (\(\beta \geq 0\)), \(\mathbf V \equiv I_d\), \(\mathbf W \equiv 0\). Let \(x_*^i\) be the (a.s. existing) limit cluster point of \(\mu^i(t)\). Then \(\mathbb P[x_*^i \neq x_*^j] = 1\) for \(i \neq j\).


Our formalization. informal
Reviewed on paper; not stated in Lean. The paper’s argument (sketch of proof, p. 30) is a symmetry/measure-theoretic one: for \(\pi\)-almost every empirical input the dynamics with \(\mathbf B \equiv \beta I_d\), \(\mathbf V \equiv I_d\), \(\mathbf W \equiv 0\) converge to a Dirac \(\delta_X\), defining a map \(f : \mathscr S_n \to \mathbb S^{d-1}\). Because the flow and the law \(\pi\) are \(\mathbb O(d)\)-equivariant, the law \(\nu = f_\#\pi\) of the limit is rotation-invariant, hence non-atomic — an atom at \(x_0\) of mass \(p\) would force an atom of mass \(p\) at every \(\mathsf R x_0\), contradicting \(\nu(\mathbb S^{d-1}) = 1\) once enough distinct rotations are chosen. Two independent inputs then give independent limits with this common non-atomic law, so \(\mathbb P[x_*^i = x_*^j] = \int \nu(\{x\})\,\mathrm d\nu(x) = 0\). We do not state this in Lean: it would require Haar measure on \(\mathbb O(d)\), the equivariant \(\omega\)-limit map \(f\), and non-atomicity infrastructure outside our current axiom layer. The result is recorded as the heuristic that the generic switch complexity is \(O(1)\) rather than the \(O(d\,N)\) of Theorem 1.1.

title: Claim graph
text:
Every result in this project is a claim in a Conventional Knowledge Commits graph: a node whose colour is its effective status (machine-checked, axiomatised, or open) and whose edges are the Depends-On / Closes relations recorded in the commit trail. A claim’s effective status is the weakest of its own status and everything it rests on, so a single open leaf colours every result above it – the graph cannot flatter the work.
Click a node to trace what it depends on; press Play (or drag the slider) to replay the commits in order and watch each claim’s status resolve as the formalization advances. The view is generated from git by claimgraph and embedded below; the same data drives the compact history timeline.








The status colours are kernel-honest: they are derived from #print axioms over each node’s Lean name by bin/axiom-report, the same source that drives the blueprint badges. A node is green only if its Lean compiles with no sorry and no project axiom.

title: About, attribution, and errata
text:
What the formalization strengthens

F1 – a sign error in the gate of Lemma B.2
The proof of Lemma B.2 (Appendix B, p. 31, eq. (B.4)) takes parameters \(U = -z\mathbf 1^\top\), \(b = \cos(R)\mathbf 1\), giving the gate \((\cos R - \langle z,x\rangle)_+\), and asserts it is positive exactly on the ball \(\mathcal B_0 = B(z,R)\). But \[\cos R - \langle z,x\rangle > 0 \iff \langle z,x\rangle < \cos R \iff d_g(z,x) > R \iff x \notin \mathcal B_0,\] so the printed gate is active on the complement of \(\mathcal B_0\); as written, the interior mass sees a zero gate and the lemma transports nothing. The fix is a one-sign typo: \(U = +z\mathbf 1^\top\), \(b = -\cos(R)\mathbf 1\). The error is typographical and recoverable – no theorem (Prop 2.2, Theorems 1.1, 1.2) is affected once the sign is flipped.
It is corroborated three ways: (i) the paper’s own Proposition 4.2, Step 3 (p. 22) uses the same gate and states it with the correct (opposite) side; (ii) the kernel-checked leaf MeasureToMeasure.Leaves.gate_pos_iff_dist (L2) proves the gate is active on \(\{d_g(z,x)>R\}\); and (iii) experiment E1 failed (mass fraction \(\approx 0.27\)) when seeding mass inside \(B(z,R)\) as the printed (B.4) suggests, and passed (fraction \(1.0\)) only on the gate-active region L2 identifies.
The full erratum note (written so it can be sent to the authors as a courtesy) is in ERRATA.md.


F2 – a rigor gap in Proposition 3.1’s induction
The induction proving Proposition 3.1 asserts the step “disjoint geodesic hulls \(\Rightarrow\) non-colinear barycenters” without proof. The formalization supplies it as the machine-checked leaf barycenter_noncolinear_of_disjoint_hull (L11) for the empirical regime, and barycenter_noncolinear_of_disjoint_hull_general (L11\('\), via Mathlib’s Convex.integral_mem) for arbitrary probability measures, with no new axioms.
